product.title: Sharf
product.body:
Sharf is an editorial type family with an experimental touch and unpredictable contrast changes between text and headline styles. Text styles are rather calm and conventional with elegant character, whereas the headline styles surprise with their eccentricity.

Sharf marries many ideas that perhaps should not exist next to each other in any kind of harmony, but we believe that many different narrations, shapes, beings can co-exist at different extremes of the design spectrum; and what is in-between might be an interesting surprise.

Silverspoon is a typeface that performs well at medium sizes. Its flared serifs, tight spacing, and compact vertical proportions make it look very stable. The user has the option to turn on slightly more curly alternates, which break the blockiness of the text setting, adding a touch of panache for those who need it. Silverspoon Condensed is a space-saving version its older cousin, which can be used for headlines and is expecially attractive for setting large quotes and logos on packages.

product.title: Method
product.body:
Method is a geometric sans-serif typeface inspired by Lining Gothic No.82. Its low contrast and geometric foundation create a clean, minimal aesthetic, while high-waisted proportions in round characters distinguish it from typical geometric designs. With its open counters and clean design, the overall appearance feels airy and fresh.

The journey began with an intriguing ‘g’ from Barnhart Bros. & Spindler’s Specimen No.9. This character’s unique signature—a compact rounded counter above a flat base, crowned by a long 90-degree ear. The design amplifies the original specimen’s geometric character, visible in the right-angled strokes of t, f, r, y, and j, while the near-perfect circles of o, b, d, p, and q reinforce its systematic approach.

Method’s high-waisted proportions appear throughout the family—not only in letters with counters and horizontal bars like B, P, R, E, F, and H, but also in round letters like C and S, where apertures sit above their visual center. This creates a cohesive high-waisted appearance across the family. The terminals of the rounds are cut straight horizontally, adding sharpness and character to letters with open counters, like C, G, S, a, c, s, 3, 6, and 9—becoming a distinctive feature of Method.

Originally based on Lining Gothic No.82’s Medium weight, Method has expanded into a comprehensive family spanning from Thin to Black weights, offering versatility for both text and display applications.

product.seo_description: Method is a geometric sans with a low contrast, open counters & horizontally cut terminals. The typeface combines precision & clarity with a fresh modern feel, perfect for both display and text applications: logotypes, brand identities, posters, etc.

product.title: Min Sans Stencil
product.body:
Min Sans Stencil is a high-contrast sans-serif typeface that transforms the elegant, calligraphy-influenced structure of the original [Min Sans](https://contemporarytype.com/fonts/min-sans) into a more robust, industrial, and urban design. Released in 2023, this variant uses a modular stencil framework, introducing precisely placed breaks in the letterforms. These mechanical cuts create a dynamic visual tension, offering an architectural and utilitarian tone while retaining the parent family’s sophistication and clean contours. It functions exceptionally well in display settings like signage, contemporary branding, and editorial layouts where a bold, graphic character is desired, and is available in six weights with accompanying italics. 

Related font family: [Min Sans](https://contemporarytype.com/fonts/min-sans)

product.title: Paragraf
product.body:
Paragraf is a serif typeface with slightly narrow proportions, a high-waisted structure, and a tall x-height, creating an elegant yet practical tone. Its clean, straight lines and sharp cuts lend a clear, rational personality, making it well-suited for editorial use.
The design emphasizes geometric simplicity, with rectangular and parallelogram shapes evident in details like the terminals of ‘r’ and the tails of ‘y.’ The bottom serifs on characters such as ‘a,’ ‘d,’ and ‘u’ reflect this geometric approach. Strokes like the tail of ‘Q,’ the legs of ‘R,’ ‘K,’ and ‘k,’ and the ear of ‘g’ are rendered with crisp, structured forms.
Horizontally and vertically cut terminals on thin strokes, seen in ‘c,’ ‘e,’ and ‘t,’ add a sharp, confident finish. The 90-degree bracketed serifs, with their smooth curves connecting to the main strokes, balance modern precision with a touch of classic elegance.

product.title: Rondeau
product.body:
Rondeau is a serif typeface rooted in mid 19th century French Elzevirs. Often cited as the first revivals in type history, Elzevirs offered an alternative to the immensely popular Didones by drawing inspiration from historic forms, namely the Roman inscriptions and European Renaissance type. In addition to acknowledging the work of Louis Perrin and his peers at the time, Rondeau takes cues from type that was clearly influenced by French Elzevirs closer to the 20th century. Typefaces like the Post/Canton Series and French Old Style (Barnhart Brothers & Spindler), Römische Antiqua (Genzsch & Heyse 1888) and Romanisch Antiqua (Schelter & Giesecke 1895) informed its development.

Due to its high contrast and dense spacing Rondeau is intended for rather large sizes. The lighter weights are delicate yet poised, maintaining the clarity and grace of the original Elzevirs, whereas the darker weights lack the typical brittleness of heavy display serif typefaces. There is still tons of contrast and sharpness to go around, but without compromising the overall robustness. Steep and narrow 21 degree italics are not based on any specific historical model. Even though there are evident cursive features, Rondeau’s italics are more rational and closely tied to the design of its upright counterpart.

It also comes with all the signature features of the **Vanilla collection** — smart stylistic alternates (a, g, l, G, J), arrows, circled numerals, and a full A–Z set of pattern glyphs. product.title: FT Together
product.body: FT Together was originally conceived as the corporate typeface for the identity of the 2022 Alliance Graphique Internationale Congress in Trieste, a port city in northeastern Italy. Inspired by the stencil lettering found on port signage, used to mark shipping containers and cargo vessels, the typeface translates that industrial aesthetic into a crafted typographic system. Its octagonal structure and the careful design of its stencil cuts give it a distinctive, functional presence, making it particularly effective for titles and short texts. The typeface is uppercase-only and available in a single weight.

product.body: Softcore is a warm, contemporary serif typeface rooted in 18th century German-Dutch typography. Its construction is inspired by J.M. Fleischmann’s work. It has a strong core with a soft character, a sensation of ink spreading on paper. Despite historical roots, its shapes have been reinvented for the digital realm. Its soft, rounded strokes are an effect of sketches made with a modern tool, a rounded, felt tip marker. The shapes were digitised with precise outlines that thrive in display sizes and optimised for smaller and longer texts, where it keeps legibility and a balanced texture. The family consists of a whole range of weights, from Thin to Black, with corresponding Italics, available in static and variable formats.

product.title: Stick
product.body:
Stick is a witch’s charm, a bonfire or a toothpick. Stick is a one-weight geometric display typeface which mimics the manual act of adhering pieces of wood together. It is drawn by the formal rule that whenever two lines intersect they must be “sharpened” to the point of almost “snapping.” This could be considered a primitive ink trap-concept that merges a physical construction-principle with a decidedly digital implementation.

Stick is—given its weight and style—excellently suited for headline usage, but in smaller sizes it demonstrates an interesting texture that justifies its use for short paragraphs as well.

product.title: Swiss NG
product.body:
Designed by Ian Party, Swiss NG is a bold reimagining of the Neo-Grotesque genre—clean, rational, and quietly radical. Drawing direct inspiration from iconic typefaces like Univers, Unica, and the wider Swiss design tradition, Swiss NG does more than pay homage—it moves the genre forward.

At its core, Swiss NG embraces the structural clarity and geometric restraint of mid-century Grotesques. But where traditional Neo-Grotesques prioritized neutrality, Swiss NG dares to express a subtle personality—refined, contemporary, and confident. With its perfectly calibrated rhythm and proportions, this typeface is designed for visual clarity at every size, making it as effective in editorial design as it is in digital interfaces.

The full type family includes 6 bundles, each composed of 22 meticulously crafted styles—from razor-sharp Hairlines to commanding Extra Blacks. Each bundle also includes its own dedicated Variable Font. With a total of 132 styles, plus a versatile Variable Font, Swiss NG offers a typographic system that adapts fluidly to your creative vision.

Whether you’re setting a bold headline, crafting immersive UI, or laying out structured information across print and screen, Swiss NG delivers. Available in OTF and WOFF2, it’s built for performance and elegance across every platform.

Swiss NG isn’t just a typeface—it’s a typographic toolset. A modern classic in the making that bridges heritage and innovation, precision and warmth, form and function.

Timeless. Technical. Swiss NG.

product.title: Fig Grotesk
product.body:
Fig Grotesk is a playful and approachable sans-serif typeface that blends historical charm with modern geometry. Inspired by a 19th-century specimen from the Amsterdam Type Foundry—specifically the small size of Breede Magee Antieke—the original letterforms featured softly integrated terminals that lost their thick-and-thin contrast. This unique detail, which lent the type an unusually affable look, served as the foundation for Fig Grotesk’s design.

In the reinterpretation process, these subtle curves were translated into a geometric framework, resulting in a sans serif that feels both cleanly contemporary and warmly expressive. Fig Grotesk follows modern grotesque proportions, but its rounded terminals introduce a touch of softness that distinguishes it from more austere counterparts.

With eight weights—Thin to Black—and matching italics across a total of 16 styles, Fig Grotesk is well-suited for branding, logos, headlines, packaging, and editorial subheads. It offers the clarity of a grotesque sans with an added layer of charm and personality.

A friendly grotesque with historical roots and contemporary finesse, Fig Grotesk brings warmth and playfulness to modern typography.

product.seo_description: Fig Grotesk is a modern sans-serif typeface with a friendly, curvy personality. Inspired by 19th-century Dutch specimens, it features geometric structure and soft terminals. Available in 8 weights and 16 styles—ideal for logos, headlines & branding.

product.title: Looklyn
product.body:
Looklyn is a contemporary slab serif typeface with a geometric foundation and a distinctive serif rhythm. Its most notable feature is the unconventional weight distribution: extra-thin vertical serifs paired with bold horizontal strokes create a lively contrast and a unique thick-and-thin texture rarely seen in slab designs.

The letterforms are built on a structural skeleton, but softened with open diagonals and subtle humanist touches inspired by handwritten strokes—especially in letters like A, K, V, and Y. This blend of architectural precision and organic gestures gives Looklyn a warm, approachable personality within a modern typographic framework.

Available in five weights—Light, Book, Medium, Bold, and Black—across both uprights and italics, Looklyn offers a total of 10 styles. Its balanced proportions and rhythmic construction make it well-suited for branding, editorial layouts, logotypes, and packaging where both structure and friendliness are key.

Looklyn brings a fresh interpretation to slab serif typography, combining clarity, charm, and flexibility.

product.title: 00 Eckmania
product.body:
00 Eckmania is a digital interpretation of “Eckmannschrift”, a 1900 typeface designed by Otto Eckmann (punches cut by Louis Hoell) and published at the Rudhardsche foundry (under the supervision of Karl Klingspor).
Although there are many digitisations, there was still a missing revival, faithful to the original yet redesigned and adapted to contemporary aesthetics and uses. Until now!
Instead of focusing on a single source, 00 Eckmania encapsulates a whole variety of tones of voices through its optical size axis.
The small size extreme can actually hold surprisingly well at text sizes and withstand relatively long pieces of text; while the display cuts show their full potential of expressiveness and flourishes.
The terminals seemingly morph from serif-like shapes to pen brush strokes creating a wide array of styles.
Two more axes to go? Unpublished drawings from Otto Eckmann for “Eckmann-Kursiv” and “Eckmann Initialen” could be very interesting historical sources to to draw inspiration from for potential Italic and Monospace axes. So this is only the first chapter!

product.title: FL Rare
product.body:
FL Rare is an efficient grotesk fused with traces of handwriting. Its clean shapes, closed apertures and low contrast place the typeface in the class of functional sans-serifs, but then it breaks from the expected with the subtle artifacts of pen movements and cursive cuts, uncommon for the genre. That eclectic approach provides both utility and flair, contemporary feel and readability.

The Cursive styles deepen the fusion further. Obvious for serif fonts, such true italics are seldom seen among grotesques, which, perhaps, makes FL Rare a surreal take on the idea of a “sans-serif cursive”. This part of the family is designed to widen the range of typographic expressions, while maintaining a consistent feel. The cursive letterforms closely resemble the upright counterparts, making them work together naturally. Sidenote: the term “cursive” is used loosely here. Naming is hard.

The family consists of 7 weights with corresponding 16° italics. It is tuned to work at different optical sizes, organized into 3 subfamilies. The Display subfamily is crisp, sharp and tightly spaced, ideal for high-impact use at large scales. The Text subfamily on the other end features more robust shapes, open apertures and loose spacing, making it comfortable to read at smaller sizes. For anything in-between, the Deck subfamily is the default choice. With its OpenType style sets, the typeface includes extra-legible alternatives for l and I, as well as single-story versions of a and g.

FL Rare comes with variable fonts corresponding to each bundle of styles, which allow to fine-tune the exact optical size, weight or italic angle, as well as to create animations or interactive transitions on the web. Cursive cuts form a separate variable font for now. FL Rare supports many Latin-based languages.

product.title: BRASS
product.body:
The BRASS display family consists of several members. There are two main sub-families: one with the “asterisk element”, in outline and “Filled” version, and the “Plain” exclusively in outline. Both outline versions are available in various stroke thicknesses.
From a design and aesthetic point of view, the typeface is linked to various references from the history and practice of graphic design. The strokes that make up the letters are influenced by experiments done with Adobe Illustrator’s digital brushes. This is visible, for instance, in the “asterisk element” that connotes the typeface, a stylization of the glitches frequent when applying digital brushes to vector strokes. However, starting with its name, the typeface is intended to be a tribute to the artistic career of Italian director Tinto Brass. Brass’ cinema, in fact, in addition to his experiments on a formal level, in editing, photography, set design and costumes, as well as his contribution to the maturation of the erotic genre in Italy in particular, can also be analyzed for his constant dialogue with graphic design and typography. Design: Franziska Weitgruber + Michele Galluzzo

product.title: RR Opague
product.body:
*Opague* is a synthetically distilled postmodern geometric sans. By virtue of a subdued color palette and an almost anonymous tastefulness *Opague* exults the ability to dominate or submit in either big or small sizes simultaneously. </br>          *Opague*’s geometry and apertures are a negotiation of historical references¹ and an insatiable appetite for brutal simplicity — not to mimic its past but to extend its future. *Opague*’s character is shaped by a circular “O” and enforced horizontal lines composed by the apertures and middle height. These punctuated lines are sharp and exacting. They seem to forcefully hold everything in place, as if trying to create a brutal order where none is needed.
</br>          These shenanigans span across a wide range of nine weights, each with a corresponding italic at a moderately steep 15° angle. The variable font format expands this 18-style banger into an even greater typographic toolbox, making it an almost infinite kaleidoscope of options beyond the static weights and angles.
</br>          *Opague* doesn’t ask for your approval. It doesn’t demand anything. It simply is. And yet, in its quiet insistence, it forces itself into the spaces where it’s needed. Whether on takeaway lightbox menus or sophisticated wine lists, intricate interface designs or plain headlines, overarching movie titles or closing credits, vast record covers or intimate lyric sheets; *Opague* is at the service of your every day design needs.
</br>          *Opague* is loaded for bear! With over 1000 glyphs per style, supporting Greek, Cyrillic, and Latin scripts, *Opague* speaks more than 280 languages. Additionally, it’s brimming with typographic tools you never knew you needed; arrows, symbols, shapes, additional brackets, all kinds of stylistic sets and contextual alternates, case-sensitive punctuation and a countless amount of sets of figures. All of that just to make sure your typography doesn’t just work, but thrives. 
</br>
</br>You’d better be ready to fall in love!
</br>
</br> ¹         Bernard Brother & Spindler’s “Lining Gothic nº82” /  Bauer Type Foundry’s “Venus” / MacKellar, Smiths & Jordan Co. “Gothics”  
</br></br>
</br></br>

product.title: RR Repose
product.body:
*Repose* is a Venn diagram of dynamic proportions and technical restraint in one beautiful sans-serif composition.
</br>          *Repose* is a simple but complex sounding collection of six variations of a single uppercase-only light weight. The differences between these variations are subtle yet powerful. While roundness decreases incrementally from everywhere to nowhere in three styles, two distinct spacing approaches will give you more options or chaos to embrace. Whatever suits you.
</br>          Though *Repose*’s general proportions are expressively dynamic, they stem from a structured foundation; the combination of four fixed widths. “O” doubles “H” doubles “I”.
The first sub-collection follows the previously mentioned spacing approach and is called “Quad”. Much like a Monospace, this “Quadrospace” — if you will, because I do — forgoes kerning in favour of simplicity and a technical awkwardness that exudes undeniable charm. But, you know … with MORE fun.
</br>The second, more conventional spacing approach is called “Prop”. It maintains the same dynamic proportions but with minor adjustments and one magical ingredient; kerning! This allows for tight typesetting and the creation of a modest elegance.
</br>          Both sub-collections come in three styles: “A” features roundings on all edges. “B” applies roundings sparingly, only on inside corners. And “C” disregards them entirely and strolls proudly bald through the typographical landscapes.
</br>          *Repose* demands to be used big and ostentatiously. There’s no need for restraint — show them what you’ve got! Whether it’s big movie titles, spread out on record covers, building-sized banners, or haughty headlines, *Repose* has you covered.
</br>          To maximize its limited range, REPOSE is packed to the gills with features! This 630+ glyph powerhouse supports more than 280 languages in both Cyrillic and Latin scripts. It offers a wide range of stylistic sets for alternative letterforms, six sets of brackets(!), an assortment of arrows and symbols, black- and white-circled figures as well as squared alternatives, and smart contextual alternates that do the heavy lifting for you.
</br>
</br> You want to have some fun — what are you waiting for?
</br></br>
</br></br>

product.title: Name Sans
product.body:
Name Sans is a modern interpretation of the tile mosaic name tablets of the New York City subway. The architects and craftworkers who designed and laid these tiles used a letter construction that was part geometric and part grotesque, with typographic optical corrections often either exaggerated or totally missing. Name Sans interprets these ideas into an extensive type system that is at once anonymous and full of personality, useful for everything from branding to wayfinding to digital interfaces.

The project began as a series of sketches in notebooks, drawn during daily commutes in New York, and was subsequently formalized into a typeface over about four years. The original mosaic letterforms vary from station to station, but this project harmonizes the sometimes divergent ideas into a useful, extensive type system.

Name Sans was conceived and built as a variable font family, but also includes static fonts for each important location within its extensive design ranges of Weight and Optical Size.

product.title: Keyframe
product.body:
Product of reinforced concrete and a soldering iron, Keyframe sports a monumental, architectural look. Its stems, linear and solid, were built to stay; the kind of construction that only the minuscule but repeated assaults of time could slowly begin to soften.

Keyframe is all about simplification, hints rather than definitions, like interpreting history from a few leftover traces. With regularity, often associated with such heavily constructed typefaces, broken by missing parts and unexpected extensions, words become like abstract decors, oscillating between hardness and fragility.

—

Design: Lucas Descroix

brand.title: Plain Form
brand.body:
Plain Form is an independent typographic practice, established by Lucas Descroix in 2022.

We focus on expressive letterforms, meant to be seen as much as to be read. We perceive type as a fascinating raw material for culture and strive to constantly question its established standards, drawing lines between the discipline’s rich history and its ever-expanding potential for novelties.

We also publish tangible things, including Plain Text, a printed publication for exploratory type design.

product.title: Paraiso
product.body:
Paraiso is a condensed display typeface with a constructed feel. Although influenced by 19th and early-20th century wood faces, Paraiso’s design is a fantasised reconstruction, straying away from nostalgia and precise historical references. Evoking a set of building cubes, this display typeface finds warmth in the regularity and somewhat comforting tightness of its rhythm.

The Paraiso family is coherent but not linear. It was designed as a limited but versatile palette for striking headlines or poster layouts, its blocky letterforms naturally calling for compact leading and big sizes.

Transit draws inspiration from Magnetic Ink Character Recognition (MICR) fonts, reimagining their functional origins with a rounded, retro flair. Rooted in the aesthetics of vintage computing, this typeface strikes a balance between precision and personality. With eight distinct widths and a complete alternative stylistic set featuring specular counters and exaggerated drop terminals, Transit brings rhythm and energy to your logos and titles. The variable version adds even more versatility, enabling smooth animations along the width axis. 

For customer support and special licenses, please contact type@morula.xyz

product.title: Grandmaster
product.body:
Built like a freestyle over a regular beat, the very condensed Grandmaster explores the border between text and abstraction. 

The extremely vertical structure, the regularity of its shapes and counter-shapes make it an almost-kinetic titling typeface, perfect for any incisive editorial layout. If you lose yourself in its rhythm you can almost hear it: cutting, scratching, mixing.

—

Design: Lucas Descroix

brand.title: Plain Form
brand.body:
Plain Form is an independent typographic practice, established by Lucas Descroix in 2022.

We focus on expressive letterforms, meant to be seen as much as to be read. We perceive type as a fascinating raw material for culture and strive to constantly question its established standards, drawing lines between the discipline’s rich history and its ever-expanding potential for novelties.

We also publish tangible things, including Plain Text, a printed publication for exploratory type design.

product.title: Michaux
product.body:
Like a profile spotted in the clouds or in the traces left by woodworms, Michaux was not created so much as it was found. Selected out of thousands of computer-generated three-stroke signs, this typeface not only proposes a radical process and a stand on the role of the designer, it offers a visual system that is as wild as it is coherent.
 
Reminiscent of dynamic handwritten styles, Michaux leaves all preconceived rules behind. It is a balancing act of freedom and stability, abstraction and legibility, that makes for an expressive and ever-surprising typeface, yielding a sort of impressive spirit that only chaos is capable of.

—

Design: Benjamin Dumond

Team: Lucas Descroix

product.title: Nostra
product.body:
Nostra plays with a feeling of satisfaction: how a few thin strokes can create the shape of a letter, either cut in a solid mass or traced in the air. Its monospaced width makes Nostra almost pattern-like, half-way between a legible and an abstract system, a block of text and an optical illusion.

Nostra presents an atypical couple only brought together by their radical proportions. The Sett is heavy and stable like a brick wall when the Stream, inspired by flourished scripts, graffiti and snakes in jars, is light and all in curves.

—

Design: Lucas Descroix

Consulting on Cyrillic: Ilya Ruderman & Yury Ostromentsky

Consulting on Greek: George Triantafyllakos

product.title: FL Prefere
product.body:
Prefere takes its deep historical roots in day 22 of running out of ideas in a challenge to make a quick font every day, back in 2021. “Hold the line” seemed silly enough. Forcing elements to align along an imaginary horizontal resulted in flat counters, brutal geometry and some blind letters. Edgy! Fast forward to 2025, it is now a complete type family, ready to raise some eyebrows.

The typeface is, of course, meant for big-sizes: posters, headlines, websites, and so on — you know the drill. Loosen up its very tight letter spacing, and it can handle somewhat smaller texts too. The family comes in 5 weights and corresponding italics, which were especially fun to design.

The name Prefere comes from the extensive process of combining letters which align among the line. The rule number one is known: the typeface’s name must look good when set in itself. It is pronounced halfway between the English “prefer” and French “préférée”, with some doubt in the voice. Prefere was fun to design and I hope you’ll have fun using it.

product.title: FL Science Sans
product.body:
Contrasting the scientific no-nonsense approach with the open-minded imagination of sci-fi, Science Sans is a clean humanist sans pushing the limits. From its highly legible Regular style, it goes surprisingly far in the dimensions of weight and width, playing with the reader a bit. Inherently humanistic and optimistic by nature, science often goes hand in hand with technologies, improving our lives. Science Sans is my purely fictional attempt to find the common typographic ground between techie startup brandings and popsci magazine covers, between futuristic movie titles and robot manufacturer websites, between the bright future and the reality we’re dealing with.

Designed to help communicating complex topics to wide audiences coherently, the typeface is universal for a whole spectrum of projects. Its widely open letterforms make Regular comfortable to read in texts and headlines; the range of styles however goes far beyond into outer space, giving a lot of options for more expressive typesetting. The typeface comes with extra-legible alternatives for I and l, as well as stylistic alternates for a, g and R.

The vertical proportions of Science Sans are adaptive to its width. The x-height is average in normal and wide styles, which helps readability and font pairing. Compressing the width, however, makes lowercase letters taller and extenders shorter, which you may find useful for tight spacing commonly needed in posters, headlines and other big-size uses.

The font family comes in 162 styles: 9 widths × 9 weights × 15° italics. It is packed with useful symbols, arrows and OpenType features such as case-sensitive forms, alternatives, fractions, superiors, tabular figures and slashed zero. Of course, all that is also available in the variable font format, which you can get with each sub-family or the full-family. Science Sans supports a lot of Latin-based languages.

Related font family: [FL Art Grotesk](https://contemporarytype.com/fonts/art-grotesk)

product.title: FL Art Grotesk
product.body:
Influenced by the atmosphere of contemporary art museums, FL Art Grotesk is a clean neo-grotesk gone extreme. Starting from the familiar Regular, the type family pushes its weights and widths beyond the norm, becoming almost ornamental in its most narrow, wide, thick and thin cuts.

In artistic spaces, everything feels different: light, objects, people, air, typography. Art Grotesk is my impressionist endeavor of capturing the elusive essence of the moment of navigating through a gallery, reading artwork captions, enjoying beautiful exhibition posters or browsing through a hip bookshop nearby, without the intent to actually buy anything.

The typeface is designed to be flexible and easy to use for anything from normal typesetting to fitting text in a limited space, from going dramatically wide for a captivating look to squeezing it all into a typographic pattern. The spectrum of weights also goes far: the common Bold, at which you’d expect it to stop, is just in the middle of its range going from Thin to Dense. The typeface comes with extra-legible alternatives for I and l, as well as as stylistic alternates for a, g, r, G and R.

Art Grotesk’s vertical proportions adapt depending on the width. In normal and wide styles, the x-height is average, making it comfortable to read and pair with other fonts. As you squeeze the width however, aiming for headlines, posters and other big-size usage, the lowercase letters grow large and extenders shrink, allowing for a much tighter spacing.

The font family comes in 162 styles: 9 widths × 9 weights × 15° italics. It is packed with useful symbols, arrows and OpenType features such as case-sensitive forms, alternatives, fractions, superiors, tabular figures and slashed zero. Of course, all that is also available in the variable font format, which you can get with each sub-family or the full-family. Art Grotesk supports a lot of Latin-based languages.

Related font family: [FL Science Sans](https://contemporarytype.com/fonts/science-sans)
